title: XLOOKUP
summary: XLOOKUP is the modern replacement for VLOOKUP and HLOOKUP. It searches a range for a value and returns a corresponding result, looking in any direction with a built-in not-found handler.
jobUseCases: Looking up an employee's department from an HR master sheet by their ID
Pulling a product price into an invoice based on the product code
Returning a customer's region from a lookup table for a sales report
Finding the latest status of an order by searching from the bottom up
Matching candidates to interview scores stored in a separate sheet

examples.title: Basic lookup
examples.explanation: Searches employee ID 'E102' in A2:A100 and returns the matching value from C2:C100. The lookup and return arrays are separate, so columns can be in any order.

examples.title: Handling not found gracefully
examples.explanation: Returns the name for the ID in G2, but if no match exists it shows 'Not Found' instead of a #N/A error. The if_not_found argument removes the need for IFERROR.

examples.title: Approximate match for tax brackets
examples.explanation: match_mode -1 finds the largest value less than or equal to the income in F2, ideal for tiered rates. Pass an empty if_not_found by leaving the slot blank.

examples.title: Search from the bottom for latest entry
examples.explanation: search_mode -1 scans from the last row upward, returning Amit's most recent status when the same name appears multiple times.

commonMistakes: Swapping lookup_array and return_array, which returns the wrong column of data
Using lookup_array and return_array of different lengths, causing a #VALUE! error
Assuming approximate match by default; XLOOKUP defaults to exact match (match_mode 0)
Skipping the if_not_found slot incorrectly when also setting match_mode, which shifts arguments
Relying on XLOOKUP in older Excel versions where it is unavailable (it requires Microsoft 365 or Excel 2021)
practiceTips: Prefer XLOOKUP over VLOOKUP for new work; it is more flexible and less fragile
Use the if_not_found argument instead of wrapping the whole formula in IFERROR
Return an entire row or column by selecting a multi-column return_array (spill)
